coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of dual emission carbon dots with yellow fluorescence for ratio detection of Al3+
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xiaoxiao
Zhang, Yan
Fu, Zheng
Tian, Jiashuo
Zhang, Liuping
Li, Wanqiong
Li, Shuailiang
Cui, Fengling
coredata.dc:description: Dual-emission carbon dots with strong yellow fluorescence (Y-CDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method from p-aminophenol and ethylenediamine. Two emission centres appeared at 380 nm and 520 nm under excitation of 270 nm. After adding Al3+, the fluorescence at 520 nm decreased due to static quenching, while the fluorescence at 380 nm gradually increased. Therefore, it could be used for ratiometric detection of Al3+. In addition, the synthesized Y-CDs were concentration dependent, and the occurrence of dual emission peaks was considerably related to the concentration. When the concentration of Y-CDs decreased, the intensity of its emission peak at 380 nm gradually increased. There was an excellent linear relationship between the concentration of synthesized Y-CDs and the ratio of two emission peaks after adding Al3+, which had been used for the detection of Al3+ in actual water samples. Moreover, the synthesized Y-CDs were successfully applied to A549 cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission carbon dots
Concentration-dependent
Al3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images and (B)Particle size distribution of Y-CDs.
(A) Full survey XPS spectra (B–D) C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of Y-CDs.
(A) Full survey XPS spectra (B–E) C1s, N1s, O1s and Al2p spectra of Y-CDs after adding Al3+.
(A) Emission peaks of Y-CDs excited at 220–320 nm (B) The UV spectrum of Y-CDs.
Selectivity of Y-CDs in (A) metal ions and (B) amino acid solutions.
(A) The influence of Al3+ concentration on Y-CDs (B) The linear relationship between F380/F520 and the concentration of Al3+.
(A) Stability of Y-CDs in NaCl solution (B) Fluorescence changes of Y-CDs in different pH environments.
Contour plots of Y-CDs after dilution of (A) 10 times (B) 50 times and (C) 100 times.
(A) The change of fluorescence lifetime of Y-CDs after adding Al3+ (B) The change of UV spectra of Y-CDs after adding Al3+.
(A) Fluorescence images of A549 cells (B) Bright feld images (C) Merge images.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of dual emission carbon dots with yellow fluorescence for ratio detection of Al3+

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-emission carbon dots with strong yellow fluorescence (Y-CDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method from p-aminophenol and ethylenediamine. Two emission centres appeared at 380 nm and 520 nm under excitation of 270 nm. After adding Al3+, the fluorescence at 520 nm decreased due to static quenching, while the fluorescence at 380 nm gradually increased. Therefore, it could be used for ratiometric detection of Al3+. In addition, the synthesized Y-CDs were concentration dependent, and the occurrence of dual emission peaks was considerably related to the concentration. When the concentration of Y-CDs decreased, the intensity of its emission peak at 380 nm gradually increased. There was an excellent linear relationship between the concentration of synthesized Y-CDs and the ratio of two emission peaks after adding Al3+, which had been used for the detection of Al3+ in actual water samples. Moreover, the synthesized Y-CDs were successfully applied to A549 cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm, which have high optical stability, excellent solubility in water, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. In recent years, CDs have been widely studied by researchers and applied in various fields, such as ions detection, catalytic degradation, bioimaging, drug delivery, information encryption, and so on [1,2]. However, most CDs have only one emission centre, which is easily affected by some factors such as self-quenching, environment, probe concentration, background noise, and instrumentation, so the accuracy of the measurement results cannot be guaranteed [3,4]. In response to this problem, the researchers designed and synthesized ratiometric fluorescent probes, which eliminated the external interference and improved the accuracy and sensitivity of the determination results [5]. For example, Wang et al. [6] constructed ratio fluorescence probes by synthesizing red emission carbon dots and combining them with quercetin. The probe could be applied for ratio detection of Al3+ with a detection limit as low as 90 nM, showing high selectivity and sensitivity. Karami et al. [7] prepared dual emission carbon dots with glucose and 3-nitroaniline as precursors, which could be used for the ratio determination of Cu2+ and aspartic acid (Asp).

Aluminium (Al) is a common metal element in human life, and excessive intake of aluminium may lead to the occurrence of many diseases. For example, Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease and osteoporosis are all speculated to be related to excessive aluminium [8,9]. For this, the world health organization stipulated that the content of Al3+ in drinking water should not be higher than 7.41 μM [10]. Up to now, researchers have established many ways to detect Al3+, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometer (LC-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical methods. However, because most of these methods need expensive instruments, complex operations and high requirements for operators, their further development in rapid detection are limited [11,12]. Consequently, it is of great important to establish a low-cost, simple and rapid method for detecting Al3+.

In this paper, the concentration-dependent dual emission fluorescent carbon dots were proposed for the first time. Different from other concentration-dependent carbon dots, when the concentration of Y-CDs was reduced, the position of long-wavelength emission peaks did not change significantly, while the short-wavelength emission peaks appeared and gradually increased. The synthesized Y-CDs exhibited two emission centres, and the quantum yield was up to 10.74%. After the addition of Al3+, the emission peaks at 380 nm and 520 nm changed to different degrees. The concentration of Al3+ could be detected by fluorescence ratio detection, and the proposed method was applied to the detection of Al3+ in tap water and river water. In addition, the A549 cells were incubated with Y-CDs and successfully captured fluorescence images under laser confocal scanning microscopy, indicating that the promising applications in ratio detection of Al3+ and cell imaging of the synthesized Y-CDs.

The p-aminophenol and ethylenediamine were bought from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagents, Ltd. Trimethylol aminomethane was bought from Aladdin Group Co, Ltd. The water used in the experiment was secondary water and all reagents were of analytical grade.

Fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, LS-55) and a UV–Vis spectrometer (Agilent technology, Cary100), respectively. XPS and FT-IR spectra were recorded by a X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (ESCALAB250Xi) and a infrared spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet FTS NEXUS), respectively. The absolute quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime data were tested from the FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh).

A one-step hydrothermal method was used to prepare dual emission carbon dots with yellow fluorescence. Firstly, 0.2 g p-aminophenol and 125 μL ethylenediamine were dissolved into 15 mL of deionized water. Then, the mixed solution was added into the autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the obtained solution was centrifuged (12000 rpm, 20 min) in a high-speed centrifuge, and the supernatant was filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm). The final product was conserved at 4 °C.

Different metal cations, anions and common amino acids with a concentration of 50 μM were added to the Y-CDs solution in order to study the selective of Y-CDs. Whereafter, the change of fluorescence at 270 nm excitation was tested on a fluorescence spectrometer.

Tap water and river water were selected as the actual water samples. After testing the possible presence of Al3+ in the water sample, it was found that the content of Al3+ in the water sample was extremely low. This had extremely little effect on the test results. First, a certain concentration of Al3+ was added into the actual water samples. Then the fluorescence spectra was scanned on the fluorescence spectrometer, and the change of fluorescence intensity was recorded. At last, the concentration was calculated according to the linear equation. The tested tap water temperature was 24.2 °C with pH is 6.8. The temperature of the river water was 25.4 °C with pH is 7.1. Before the test, the water sample was centrifuged in a high-speed centrifuge for 20 min (12000 r/min). Then the water sample was filtered twice using a 0.22 μm filter membrane.

1 mL A549 cells were incubated in confocal dish for 24 h, and the cell density was about 3 × 104 cells/mL. Then, the confocal dish was further incubated with Y-CDs for 24h. Subsequently, it was washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4). Finally, the fluorescence images of A549 cells were photographed by confocal laser scanning microscope.

The morphology and particle size of Y-CDs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the prepared Y-CDs exhibited spherical particles and evenly distributed. The particle size distribution diagram was obtained by calculating 100 random particles (Fig. 1B). The results showed that the particle size of the synthesized Y-CDs was 3.53–6.52 nm, with an average particle size of 4.89 nm.

To understand the specific structure of Y-CDs, the functional groups and components elements of Y-CDs were characterized by FTIR and XPS. Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectrum of Y-CDs, the absorption peaks at 3338.42 cm−1, 3279.65 cm−1 and 2919.54 cm−1 confirmed the presence of O–H/N–H/C–H [13,14]. The peak at 1565.17 cm−1 could be assigned to –CN stretching vibration, and the peak at 1316.32 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of C–N = [15]. Moreover, CC and C–O could be found at 1471.07 cm−1 and 1228.27 cm−1, respectively [16].

Fig. 3 displays the XPS spectra of Y-CDs. Three peaks could be seen obviously from the full spectrum of Y-CDs (Fig. 3A), which demonstrated Y-CDs were composed of C, N and O at 284.8, 399.1 and 531.7eV, respectively [17]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s emerged three peaks at 284.3, 285.5 and 288.0 eV, which could be attributed to the CC/C–C, C–N/C–O and CO, respectively [18,19]. The N1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.3 eV and 401.0 eV, revealed the existence of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and N–H, respectively [15,20]. In the O1s spectrum, the CO and C–O groups were located at 530.9 eV and 532.2 eV, respectively [21]. Combining the infrared and XPS spectral data, it could be concluded that Y-CDs mainly contained C, N and O elements, and the surface functional groups were composed of hydrophilic groups such as –OH, –NH2.

By testing the XPS spectra of Y-CDs after the addition of Al3+, the full spectrum of Y-CDs after the addition of Al3+ was obtained (Fig. 4 A). It was found that there were four elements C, O, N and Al in Y-CDs. Fig. 4B shows the C1s spectrum of Y-CDs, which consisted of CC/C–C (284.67eV) and C–N/C–O (286.14eV). Fig. 4C shows the spectrum of N1s, with four distinct peaks at 399.77 eV, 401.80 eV, 406.79 eV and 407.29 eV, consisting of pyridinic N,pyrrolic N and N–H. Fig. 4D displays the O1s spectrum, consisting of C–O(532.14) and CO(532.9eV). Fig. 4E represents the Al2p spectrum, with three distinct absorption peaks at 72.56 eV,74.40 eV and 74.93 eV respectively, indicating that aluminum existed mainly in the form of metal aluminum and alumina. By analyzing the test results of XPS, it was found that the content and location of various chemical bonds on the surface of Y-CDs changed significantly, especially in the high-resolution spectrum of N1s, which might be due to the recombination of chemical bonds after the addition of Al3+ to Y-CDs and the formation of new substances.

In order to determine the optical properties of Y-CDs, the fluorescence spectra of Y-CDs were tested under different excitation. As shown in Fig. 5 A, carbon dots exhibited dual emission peaks at 380 nm and 520 nm under excitation from 220 nm to 320 nm. With the continuous increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity of the dual emission peaks also changed correspondingly. Considering the fluorescence intensity of dual emission peaks, as the excitation wavelength was finally chosen to be 270 nm for the follow-up study. In addition, the absolute quantum yield of the Y-CDs was 10.74%. Fig. 5B shows the UV spectra of Y-CDs. A narrow strong absorption peak at 231 nm was ascribed to the π-π* transition of the CC group. A distinct absorption peak was located at 296 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition [22]. And the weak broad absorption peak at 410 nm might be from the n-π* transition of the surface state [12]. The Insets are photographs of Y-CDs under natural light and 365 nm UV light.

The selective of Y-CDs was evaluated by adding diverse potentially interfering species. Different ions and amino acids with a concentration of 50 μM were added to the Y-CDs solution. Then the fluorescence intensity changes of the dual emission peaks were recorded. Fig. 6 A and 6B exhibit the changes of the ratio F380/F520 after adding different ions and amino acids. Except for the addition of Al3+, no obvious response was observed when adding other interfering substances. The results illustrated that Y-CDs exhibited outstanding selectivity for Al3+ detection.

The sensitivity of Y-CDs to Al3+ was verified by detecting the changes in fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs after adding different concentrations of Al3+. Fig. 7 A reveals the change of the emission peak intensity of Y-CDs at 380 nm and 520 nm after adding 0–10 μM Al3+. With the increased of Al3+ concentration, the emission peak intensity of Y-CDs at 380 nm gradually enhanced while the emission peak intensity at 520 nm gradually decreased, and the emission peak intensity at different positions displayed a proportional change. Therefore, the synthesized dual emission Y-CDs could be used for ratio detection of Al3+. Fig. 7B represents the linear relationship between the concentration (0–10 μM) and the fluorescence intensity change ratio F380/F520 of Y-CDs after adding Al3+. The results exhibited a strong linear relationship (the correlation coefficient R2 was 0.9955) between fluorescence intensity ratio F380/F520 and the concentration of Al3+. The linear equation was y = 0.03828x + 0.4363, and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.095 μM. Therefore, the synthesized dual emission carbon dots could be well used for ratio detection of Al3+.

Stability is also an important parameter in studying the optical properties of carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the stability of Y-CDs was studied in a high concentration of salt solution. The carbon dots maintained good stability even in salt solutions with concentrations up to 1 M/L, indicating that the synthesized Y-CDs could be better applied in physiological environment.

Fig. 8B exhibits the fluorescence intensity change curve of Y-CDs in Tris-HCl buffer solution with pH 3-10. The ratio of Y-CDs fluorescence intensity F380/F520 showed a linear relationship with pH, indicating that it also could be used for pH monitoring.

Hyo Jeong Yoo et al. [23] proposed that the distance between particles was the key factor affecting the dual emission peak. In this regard, contour plots were used to show the variation of dual emission peaks of Y-CDs at different concentrations (Fig. 9 A–C). Y-CDs had one emission center at 520 nm under excitation of 230–300 nm (Fig. 9A). When diluted 50 times with secondary water, a new emission peak appeared at 380 nm (Fig. 9B). When diluted to 100 times, we found that the intensity of the emission peak at 380 nm gradually increased (Fig. 9C). It was speculated that the concentration-dependent dual emission characteristics of Y-CDs might be related to the interaction between particles. When the concentration changed, the surface state of carbon dots would also change. At a high concentration, intermolecular forces pulled the CDs particles closer, resulting in a higher degree of aggregation. The distance between particles was short, and functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots were combined together, thus narrowing the energy gap and facilitating long-wavelength emission. The emission peak at the short wavelength might be difficult to appear because of the aggregation quenching. As the concentration decreased, the distance between particles gradually increased and the degree of aggregation decreased. There were fewer related groups on the surface of Y-CDs, which was more conducive to short-wavelength emission [24–26]. Therefore, when the concentration decreased, the emission wavelength at 380 nm gradually appeared and enhanced.

In order to study the possible quenching mechanism of Y-CDs, the fluorescence lifetime changes of Y-CDs were tested with the addition of Al3+. As shown in Fig. 10 A, the fluorescence lifetime of Y-CDs was 4.71 ns, and it was changed to 4.48 ns after the addition of Al3+. The fluorescence lifetime did not change significantly, indicating that static quenching might occurred. Moreover, Fig. 10B shows the UV spectra of Y-CDs and Al3+, and the green line represents the UV absorption spectrum of Al3+, which was not absorbed at 200–800 nm. Furthermore, it was no overlap with excitation and emission spectra of Y-CDs, which could exclude internal filtration effect and fluorescence resonance energy transfer. In addition, the absorption peaks of Y-CDs changed after adding Al3+, indicating the formation of ground-state complexes. This further demonstrated the possibility of static quenching.

To confirm the feasibility, the synthesized dual-emitting carbon dots were used to determine Al3+ in tap water and river water. The recovery of Al3+ in real samples was studied by standard addition method. Using the linear equation y = 0.03828x + 0.4363, the concentrations of Al3+ in the actual water samples were calculated. As shown in Table 1 , the dual emission carbon dots could be well used in the detection of actual water samples, and the recovery rate was shown to vary between 95% and 101% with reasonable RSD range of 0.227%–0.313%, suggesting that the established ratiometric method was reliable in the real media.

At last, the potential application of Y-CDs in bioimaging using A549 cells was investigated. Firstly, A549 cells were cultured in confocal dishes for 24 h, and then Y-CDs were added for further incubation for 24 h. Eventually, the images were taken under a confocal laser scanning microscope. The confocal fluorescence images were taken with 405 nm laser in a 480–580 nm channel (Fig. 11 A–C). Obviously, A549 cells incubated with Y-CDs showed bright yellow fluorescence, indicating that the synthesized Y-CDs could enter the cells and be used for living cell imaging.

In this paper, concentration-dependent intrinsic dual emission Y-CDs were synthesized by primary hydrothermal method. The Y-CDs had bright yellow fluorescence, stable optical properties and the quantum yield was as high as 10.74%. They were highly selective for Al3+ and could be used for ratio detection of Al3+, with detection limits as low as 0.095 μM. The proposed method had been successfully applied to detect Al3+ in tap water and river water. In addition, the Y-CDs also had potential applications prospect in pH monitoring and bioimaging.

Xiaoxiao Gao: Conceptualization, Visualization, Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yan Zhang: Supervision. Zheng Fu: Software. Jiashuo Tian: Visualization. Liuping Zhang: Visualization. Wanqiong Li: Investigation. Shuailiang Li: InvestigationFengling Cui: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Union Foundation of Henan (U1704170) and the Key Programs for Science and Technology Development in Henan Province (192102310300, 232102320219), the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Ministry of Education (20A610005, 21A180015) and the Doctor Initiated Project Funding of Henan Normal University (QD18083).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yaoping, H.; Zhijin, G.; Junfei, L. Fluorescence detection of malachite green in fish tissue using red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped carbon dots. Food Chem. 2021, 335, 127677.
